reasoning:
Tromelin Island is located in the Southern Hemisphere at roughly 15.8° S. The seasonal climate there exhibits a warming trend from mid-spring (October) through mid-summer (January). The recent known temperature on Oct 16 was approximately 25.8 °C. 

The baseline date, Oct 26, is expected to have a daily mean temperature around 26.1 °C. The standard deviation of the daily mean temperature for a maritime tropical station is characteristically small, roughly 0.6 °C. 

For November 2 (+7 days), the climatological mean marginally increases by about 0.2-0.3 °C. Factoring in variance from weather systems, the chance of it being strictly warmer than Oct 26 is roughly 60-62%.
For November 25 (+30 days), the climatological mean jumps meaningfully to ~27.0 °C. The delta of ~0.9 °C yields roughly an 80-85% probability of being higher than the baseline.
For January 24 (+90 days), it is mid-summer on Tromelin, with average temperatures peaking around 28.5 °C. Given the large delta of ~2.4 °C against a daily variability of ~0.85 °C (for the difference), it is almost a near certainty that the summer day will be statistically warmer than the mid-spring baseline day, barring an extreme and rare tropical cyclone event occurring precisely on January 24 that somehow temporarily forces the temperature equivalent to a spring average.

Although generic guidance suggests pushing longer-term event uncertainty towards 0.5, predicting seasonal climate variables is highly deterministic due to the overarching solar forcing. As a result, the distance-to-0.5 correctly increases (more certainty) alongside the forecast horizon.
